title: Question 1.1
statement_text: For which subsets $K \subset E^n$ does every red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ contain a red pair of points of distance one or a blue isometric copy of $K$?
context_text: This is the central question studied throughout the paper. The notation $E^n \to (\ell_2, K)$ means every red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ contains a red copy of $\ell_2$ or a blue copy of $K$, and $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ means there is a coloring avoiding both. The paper says it follows Erdős et al. [4] in studying this question.
notes: The theorem header carries no parenthetical attribution; the paper notes it follows Erdős et al. [4] in studying this general question.

title: Erdős–Graham–Montgomery–Rothschild–Spencer–Straus Question on $\ell_2$ vs large sets
statement_text: For every natural number $n$, is there a set $K \subset E^1$ and a red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ containing no red copy of $\ell_2$ and no blue copy of $K$? More generally, for every natural number $d$, is there a natural number $n$ depending only on $d$ such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ for every $K \subset E^d$?
context_text: Erdős et al. stressed the case $d = 1$ and stated they expected the answer to be negative. The present paper's main theorem, Theorem 1.1, shows that for $m = 10^5 n$ one has $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, \ell_m)$, answering the question affirmatively and showing $n$ must grow logarithmically in $|K|$.
attributed_to: Erdős, Graham, Montgomery, Rothschild, Spencer and Straus
paper_contribution: Answers the question affirmatively for $d = 1$: for $m = 10^5 n$ there is a red/blue-coloring of $E^n$ with no red copy of $\ell_2$ and no blue copy of $\ell_m$, contradicting the authors' expectation.
notes: PDF source — question referenced in both the abstract and Section 1; attributed to [3] (1973) and [4] (1975) in the text.

title: Open problem: $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$ for large $m$
statement_text: For every natural number $m$, does there exist a natural number $n$ such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_3, \ell_m)$?
context_text: In the concluding remarks the authors say they were unable to decide this question. They consider it unlikely to hold for large $m$ but were unable to exhibit a coloring confirming their suspicion. Erdős et al. [3] showed that $E^n \to (\ell_6, \ell_6)$ for all $n$, providing related context.
notes: PDF source — stated in prose in the concluding remarks without a labelled theorem environment.

title: Erdős–Graham–Montgomery–Rothschild–Spencer–Straus Problem on universal two-coloring bound
statement_text: For every natural number $n$, does there exist a natural number $m$ depending only on $n$ such that for every set $K \subset E^n$ of size $m$ there is a two-coloring of $E^n$ with no monochromatic copy of $K$?
context_text: This problem from Erdős et al. [4] asks whether a universal size threshold $m(n)$ exists guaranteeing a monochromatic-free two-coloring for all point sets of that size in $E^n$. By rescaling one may assume $K$ is $1$-separated, so $\ell_2 \subset K$. Theorem 1.1 gives a partial answer when the diameter of $K$ is bounded.
attributed_to: Erdős, Graham, Montgomery, Rothschild, Spencer and Straus
paper_contribution: Partially answers the problem: if the diameter of $K \subset E^n$ is at most $R$ and $|K| > 10^4 n \log R$, then a two-coloring of $E^n$ with no monochromatic copy of $K$ exists; a complete answer requires removing the dependence on the diameter $R$.
notes: PDF source — stated in concluding remarks and attributed to [4].

title: Open problem: removing diameter dependence from Theorem 1.1
statement_text: Can the dependence on the diameter $R$ be removed from Theorem 1.1? That is, does there exist a function $f(n)$, independent of the diameter of $K$, such that $E^n \not\to (\ell_2, K)$ for every $1$-separated set $K \subset E^n$ with $|K| > f(n)$?
context_text: Theorem 1.1 requires $|K| > 10^4 n \log R$ where $R$ is the diameter of $K$. The authors note in the concluding remarks that a complete answer to the Erdős et al. problem on a universal two-coloring bound would follow from resolving this problem, and describe it as interesting in its own right.
notes: PDF source — stated in prose in the concluding remarks without a labelled theorem environment.
